title: Rush coiled oval mat
subtitle: An oval coiled mat from plaited rush, suitable for a doorstep or kitchen
excerpt: An oval coiled mat worked from lengths of twisted rush. The coil is built up in an oval from the centre, each row lashed to the previous. The finished mat is 450 mm long by 300 mm wide and 12 to 15 mm thick. Soft rush or bulrush is the traditional material; seagrass cord is a ready-to-use substitute.
sourceNotes: Cooke The Basketmaker (1882) for the coiled rush mat method; Beeton's Book of Household Management (1861) for the oval kitchen mat form.

glossaryTerms.definition: A basketry construction method where a continuous rope of material is wound in a spiral and each row is lashed to the previous one.
The stems of soft rush or bulrush, gathered and dried. Used for coiled mats, chair seats, and plaited baskets.
The binding stitch that connects each new coil row to the one below. The lashing thread passes through the previous coil at regular intervals.
Immersing dried rush in water to restore flexibility before working. Over-soaked rush becomes slimy and unpleasant to handle.

content.text: A coiled mat is one of the oldest floor-covering techniques: a continuous rope of twisted
is wound in a flat oval spiral, each row bound to the previous with a simple

technique requires no frame or upright: the mat grows in your hands from the centre out. Seagrass cord (twisted three-strand, 6 mm) is the easiest starting material; fresh or dried soft rush is the traditional choice.

For a 450 by 300 mm mat you need approximately 30 metres of 6 mm seagrass cord or 500 g of dried rush twisted into a continuous rope. If using dried rush, soak the stems for ten minutes and mellow in a damp cloth for thirty minutes; then twist groups of four to six stems together to form a rope 8 to 10 mm in diameter. The lashing thread is thin seagrass or strong garden twine; a blunt-nosed bodkin threaded with 2 m of lashing thread is the only other tool needed.

briefly: ten minutes in the trough is enough. Over-soaked rush becomes translucent and slimy and is unpleasant to handle; it also shrinks more on drying and can loosen the mat. Seagrass cord does not need soaking.

Coil the first 150 mm of cord into a tight oval and lash it to itself at six points evenly spaced around the circumference to form the centre core. This oval core will be 120 mm long and 60 mm wide; it sets the long axis of the finished mat. Thread the bodkin with lashing twine. Begin the second row by laying the cord alongside the first row and stitching through the previous row at every 30 to 40 mm.

Continue adding rows, lashing each one to the previous. The oval grows by adding extra cord at each end before turning the corners. To keep the mat flat, add rows at a consistent rate and keep the lashing tension even all the way round. If the mat begins to dome upward, the outer rows are pulled too tight; ease off and re-stitch the last row if needed.

At the final row, taper the end of the cord over the last 80 mm by pulling away outer stems from the twist. Lash the tapered end down with extra stitches worked very close together so the end blends into the mat surface. Trim any protruding ends with secateurs flush to the lashing.

attrs.symptom: The mat domes upward in the centre
attrs.cause: Outer rows lashed too tight, pulling the edges down
attrs.fix: Lay the mat face-down on a flat surface and press firmly. Re-wet the mat, press flat, and allow to dry under a board with weight on top.

attrs.symptom: The lashing thread pulls through the coil
attrs.cause: Thread too thin or coil material too loosely twisted
attrs.fix: Use a doubled thread for the lashing stitches. If the coil is soft rush, twist it tighter before the next section.
